topic: Embedded - Linker Scripts and Memory Maps

turns.content: I'm trying to configure my linker script for a Cortex-M4 using the cortex-m-rt crate, but I keep getting errors about undefined symbols in the heap region. The memory.x file looks correct with FLASH and RAM sections defined, yet when I try to build with cargo, the linker complains that some data from the .bss section isn't being placed correctly.

The issue usually stems from how you define the heap size relative to the actual stack usage in your program. When defining the heap region in memory.x for cortex-m, you must ensure the end address aligns with the maximum stack requirement plus a safety margin. If your .bss section overflows into the reserved stack area or vice versa, the linker will fail because it cannot distinguish between valid data and executable code regions. Specifically, if you use the wrong alignment constraints on your heap definition, the resulting layout might violate the Cortex-M4 memory map requirements, causing undefined behavior at runtime.

That sounds plausible, but I've already added a 128-byte padding between my stack and heap in memory.x. The linker still fails with an error about overlapping regions. Is it possible that the way I'm defining the .data section is conflicting with how rustc places it? Maybe I should try using a different linker script format instead of the standard one provided by cortex-m-rt?

The problem isn't necessarily the format, but rather how you've placed the sections relative to the memory boundaries. In Rust, when you use extern crate cortex_m_rt or link against it, the compiler generates specific section symbols like .text, .data, and .bss that must land exactly where your linker script expects them. If your memory.x defines a FLASH region ending at 0x1000 but your .text section is placed beyond that boundary due to incorrect load addresses or start offsets, you'll get this overlap error regardless of the format used. The cortex-m-rt crate assumes standard conventions for these regions, so deviating from them without updating all references will break compilation.

I understand about the boundaries now, but I'm still confused why simply increasing the RAM size in memory.x isn't solving it even though my variables seem small. Is there a specific constraint on how large the heap can be relative to the stack? Also, does the linker script need to explicitly mention the vector table address for this to work properly?

Yes, there is a strict constraint: the total size of your stack plus heap must not exceed the available RAM region defined in memory.x. If you allocate more RAM than what's physically present or if your code allocates variables that span across section boundaries incorrectly, the linker will reject it. Additionally, while the vector table address isn't explicitly required in every case, the cortex-m-rt crate expects the .text section to start at a specific offset within FLASH. If you define your FLASH region starting at an incorrect address or misalign it improperly with the vector table location (typically 0x1F00 for Cortex-M4), the runtime initialization will fail because the CPU won't know where to jump during reset.

Okay, so I should align my FLASH start address properly and ensure my stack+heap fits within RAM. But what if I need dynamic allocation? Can I just use the standard Rust heap and let the linker handle it automatically without modifying memory.x at all?

You cannot rely solely on automatic handling for embedded systems because the standard Rust heap doesn't account for flash-mapped code or strict memory isolation requirements. While you can define a heap region in your linker script using macros like `__heap_start` and `__heap_limit`, simply letting cargo manage everything without configuring memory.x will result in incorrect symbol placement if your binary exceeds default assumptions. For example, if your program generates more .data than the default RAM allows, or if you need to place specific sections at custom addresses for flash-mapped code (like ROM-resident constants), manual configuration via a properly formatted linker script is mandatory.

topic: Embedded - Linker Scripts and Memory Maps

turns.content: I've read about memory.x for Cortex-M but I still don't get why the linker script is so critical when writing cortex-m-rt code. It seems like just defining sections isn't enough, right?

You're right that it's more than just defining sections. Think of the linker script as a blueprint for physical memory, not just logical data placement. When you define regions in memory.x, you are telling the build system where to put your FLASH and RAM sections relative to the actual silicon layout. If your heap region definition is wrong, the runtime might try to allocate more stack space than physically available on your microcontroller's RAM, leading to hard crashes that are impossible to debug without this script.

topic: Embedded - Linker Scripts and Memory Maps

turns.content: I'm reviewing a colleague's Cortex-M project and I noticed they are using memory.x to define the heap. They put the .bss section right after FLASH but before RAM, which seems odd to me.

That placement is actually correct for a standard layout where you want static data in RAM and dynamic allocations also in RAM, but let's clarify the order. In memory.x, FLASH comes first because it holds code, followed by .bss or .data sections which map to RAM regions, and finally heap and stack definitions.
So if I define FLASH before RAM in my linker script, does that guarantee the compiler will place static variables correctly?

Not necessarily. The order in memory.x only defines physical layout; the compiler decides where sections go based on attributes like [NOEXEC] or [RODATA]. You must explicitly mark FLASH regions with [FLASH] and RAM regions without it, otherwise you risk placing .text over writable data.
Got it. But how do I ensure the heap doesn't overlap with stack or static variables?

You need to define separate entries for each region in memory.x: FLASH, RAM for .data/.bss, and a distinct HEAP section. The linker will merge these into the final layout, so if your heap starts before your stack ends, you'll get a collision at runtime.
What about using the cortex-m-rt tooling to auto-generate this?

cortex-m-rt doesn't generate memory.x automatically; it expects you to provide one. However, some build systems like QEMU or RIOT Framework have templates that help you define FLASH/RAM/heap properly without manual errors.
I think I understand the structure now, but can you show me how to add heap_region in memory.x?

You define it like this: MEMORY { FLASH : ORIGIN(0x08000000) LENGTH(0x00100000) ; RAM : ORIGIN(0x20000000) LENGTH(0x00040000) ; HEAP : ORIGIN(0x20003000) LENGTH(0x00100000) } and then in linker script you map heap_region to that section.
Wait, I thought .bss should come before HEAP? Is the order flexible?

Yes, the order in memory.x is flexible as long as origins and lengths don't overlap. But logically, you want static data (.data/.bss) loaded first, then heap for dynamic allocation, so placing HEAP after RAM makes sense for boot sequence.
One more thing: what if I need to reserve memory for a third-party library?
You can add another section in memory.x called 'LIB_HEAP' with its own ORIGIN and LENGTH, then tell the linker script to use that section when initializing your crate's runtime data.
Ah! So I just need to make sure my heap_region definition matches exactly what the linker expects?
Exactly. If your memory.x says HEAP starts at 0x20003000 with length 4KB, but your linker script defines heap_region as starting at 0x20003800, you'll get a mismatch and potentially corrupt data.

topic: Embedded - Linker Scripts and Memory Maps

turns.content: I'm building a tool to automate some repetitive tasks on my microcontroller, but I'm getting confused about how the memory layout works. Specifically, when using cortex-m-rt, how do I define the heap region and ensure sections are placed correctly in FLASH versus RAM?

That's a great start. The key is understanding that for Cortex-M devices, you need to create a memory.x file that explicitly defines the FLASH and RAM regions using load, no-load, and define directives. For cortex-m-rt specifically, the linker script must match the chip's internal map so sections like .text go to FLASH while data goes to RAM.
Wait, didn't we say that modern compilers handle this automatically? I thought I just needed to set a few flags and let the tooling figure out where each section should live?

I think there might be a slight misunderstanding there. While high-level languages like Rust with crates like cortex-m-rt do provide helper macros for common regions, they don't replace the need to manually specify memory maps in your linker script if you want fine-grained control over section placement. The tooling helps generate code but doesn't eliminate the requirement to define FLASH and RAM regions explicitly.

You're right, I was thinking too loosely about Rust's macros handling everything. But what if I want to verify that my .text section is actually in FLASH before flashing? Is there a way to inspect the linker output directly?

Yes, you can use tools like objdump or readelf on the binary after linking to verify where sections ended up. For example, running objdump -h your_binary will show you which section maps to FLASH versus RAM based on how your memory.x defined them.

That's helpful. But I'm still worried about runtime errors if the heap size is wrong in my linker script. How do I make sure the cortex-m-rt tooling actually respects the heap region definition I put in memory.x?

The cortex-m-rt tooling does respect your memory.x definitions, but you need to ensure that the heap size variable is declared correctly in your linker script and matches what your code expects. If you define a small heap region by mistake, runtime panics can occur when trying to allocate more than available space.
Got it. So the flow is: write memory.x with FLASH/RAM regions and heap size, run cortex-m-rt tooling to generate code, then verify the final binary's section mapping before deployment?

Exactly. That workflow ensures your sections are placed correctly in FLASH versus RAM, the heap is sized properly for runtime safety, and you can validate everything with objdump before flashing to avoid costly re-flashes.

topic: Embedded - Linker Scripts and Memory Maps

turns.content: I'm trying to port my Java microservice logic into a bare-metal Rust project for an embedded device using cortex-m. I've written a simple memory.x file that defines FLASH and RAM regions with the cortex-m-rt linker script, but I'm struggling to place specific sections correctly. My current setup uses the standard heap region definition in memory.x, yet when I try to compile my main binary with the correct linker flags, it seems to be placing data in the wrong segment.

It sounds like you might be missing a critical detail about how the heap is defined relative to the FLASH and RAM regions. In your memory.x file, the heap region definition needs to align with where your Rust runtime expects the stack and data to live. For cortex-m-rt specifically, you need to ensure that the heap starts at an address within the RAM region, not overlapping with FLASH or falling into unallocated space.

I've double-checked my linker script, and it looks correct. I'm using the standard approach where I define the FLASH section for code and a separate RAM section for data. However, when I run the binary on the board, I get a hard fault at startup. It seems like the memory map isn't matching what the CPU expects.

Hard faults often indicate that the linker script doesn't account for the specific alignment requirements of your cortex-m core or that the FLASH and RAM regions are not correctly ordered. You should verify that your memory.x file defines the FLASH region first, followed by the RAM region, ensuring no gaps exist between them unless intentionally reserved. Additionally, check if you're using the correct linker flags like --defsym to map symbols to specific addresses in the generated link map.

I've tried adjusting the order of regions in memory.x, but the issue persists. It looks like my Rust code is being placed incorrectly relative to the stack pointer setup at startup. Maybe I need to rethink how I'm defining the heap region in relation to the FLASH and RAM sections?

The heap region definition should be carefully positioned within your RAM section to avoid conflicts with stack usage or data segments. In your linker script, ensure that the heap size is explicitly defined and matches the available free space in the RAM region after accounting for the stack. You might also consider using a custom memory map generator tool to visualize how sections are being placed against your FLASH and RAM regions.

I've been manually calculating offsets, which is error-prone. Is there a better way to handle this? Should I be using the cortex-m-rt specific linker script templates instead of writing my own memory.x file from scratch?

Using built-in linker scripts like those provided by the cortex-m-rt ecosystem can save time, but they may not perfectly match your specific hardware constraints. It's often better to start with a template and then adjust the FLASH and RAM regions based on your actual memory map. This way, you retain control over how sections are placed while leveraging tested patterns from existing projects.

I've started modifying a standard template, but I'm still confused about how to properly define the heap region in memory.x without breaking my Rust compilation. Can you give me some specific examples of what the correct structure should look like for a typical cortex-m device?

A good example would be defining FLASH first, then RAM with explicit start and end addresses, followed by a heap region that sits comfortably within the RAM boundaries. Ensure you're using the correct syntax for section placement in your linker script so that Rust's optimizer knows where to place its data segments.

I think I understand now, but there's one thing that still doesn't click. Why does my current setup fail when I try to compile the binary? Is it possible that the linker script isn't telling Rust where the actual memory layout is?

Yes, exactly! The linker script must provide clear guidance on how sections are placed in FLASH and RAM regions for Rust to generate the correct binary. If your memory.x file doesn't align with the expectations of the cortex-m-rt runtime or if there's a mismatch between the defined heap region and actual available memory, the resulting binary will fail at startup due to incorrect stack or data placement.

That makes sense. So I need to ensure my linker script accurately reflects the physical memory layout of my device. Once I fix this, should I be able to compile my Rust code successfully and see it run on the board?

Yes, once your memory.x file correctly defines FLASH and RAM regions with a proper heap region definition, compiling your Rust binary should succeed without hard faults. You'll also notice that the sections are placed in their intended locations, allowing your application to execute properly on the embedded device.

topic: Embedded - Linker Scripts and Memory Maps

turns.content: I've always thought of memory management in embedded systems like how the Rust runtime manages the heap, but I'm wondering if the linker script plays a similar role to std::alloc::System for managing raw memory regions.

That's an excellent comparison. While Rust's System is about allocation logic at compile time or runtime, the linker script in embedded systems like cortex-m acts as a map that tells the compiler exactly where each section of code and data should reside in physical memory before any execution happens.
So when I look at memory.x for a Cortex-M, it's not just defining regions like FLASH and RAM, but also dictating how the cortex-m-rt code gets placed into those specific areas?

Exactly. The memory.x file defines the layout of your flash and RAM segments, specifying where instructions live versus where variables reside. This ensures that when you compile with cortex-m-rt, the resulting binary respects these physical boundaries.
I see how that helps avoid conflicts, but what about defining a heap region? How does that differ from just allocating in standard C?

In embedded systems, the heap is often explicitly defined within your linker script by creating a dedicated RAM segment. Unlike Rust's automatic allocation which might involve some overhead or runtime checks, this approach requires manual configuration to ensure enough space exists for dynamic allocations without overwriting critical sections.
I understand the necessity of careful placement now, but I'm curious about how this relates to section attributes and their impact on performance or security in a real scenario.

Section attributes like read-only or executable flags are crucial for ensuring that the FLASH region remains intact during execution while preventing accidental writes to sensitive areas. This is similar to how Rust uses traits to enforce safety guarantees at compile time, but here you're doing it through explicit memory layout constraints.
It sounds like there's a direct parallel between the way Rust enforces type safety and how linker scripts enforce memory safety. Is that accurate?

Yes, you've hit on a key insight. While Rust uses traits and types to prevent misuse at compile time, the linker script prevents physical memory corruption by enforcing boundaries before code runs. Both are essential for building reliable systems.

That analogy really helps me visualize it. So if I were to think of Rust's safety as a software layer, the linker script acts like the hardware foundation that ensures everything fits properly underneath?

Perfect analogy. You're thinking about this correctly. The linker script provides the structural integrity needed for your code to run safely on constrained hardware without crashing or corrupting data.

topic: Embedded - Linker Scripts and Memory Maps

turns.content: I'm reviewing the memory layout for our new cortex-m project. What's the standard structure in memory.x?

For a typical cortex-m setup, you define FLASH and RAM regions in memory.x before adding sections. You place the .text section at the start of FLASH and the .data section immediately after it, then add RAM for variables and heap. The key is ensuring your linker script matches the chip's flash map so the binary doesn't overflow boundaries.
Got it, but how do I handle the heap region definition? Should I use a fixed size or dynamic allocation?

You define the heap in memory.x as a separate RAM region with a specific start and end address. For most projects using cortex-m-rt, you set a static limit to prevent fragmentation, then allocate from that pool at runtime. If you need more flexibility later, you can switch to dynamic allocation by adjusting the linker constraints on the heap section.
That sounds safer for now. But what if I want to place sections differently? Can I override the default order?

Yes, you can reorder sections in memory.x by defining them explicitly before adding the .text or .data entries. You might also use linker constraints like `L_GCC` flags to force specific placement without changing the script itself. This helps when you need to separate code from data for power management or flash mapping.
Interesting. What about the difference between using a standard linker vs. something like ldscript? Does that affect how I handle sections?

The standard toolchain uses a built-in linker script generator, while custom scripts give you full control over memory maps. For most embedded Rust projects using cortex-m-rt, you stick with the default unless you need non-standard flash layouts or multiple banks. Custom scripts are useful when you want to optimize for specific hardware constraints like limited flash space.
Okay, so I should just use the default unless I have a reason not to? Is that correct?

Correct. Unless your chip has unusual memory requirements or you're targeting multiple configurations with different maps, stick with the standard layout. You can always refactor later if performance tuning demands it.
One more thing: what happens if I accidentally define overlapping regions in memory.x? Does that break compilation?

Yes, overlapping regions cause linker errors because the toolchain detects conflicting address ranges. The build will fail at the linking stage before even generating a binary. You'll need to fix the script by adjusting start/end addresses or removing duplicate definitions.
Thanks for clarifying that. So I should double-check my memory.x file against the chip datasheet before building.
Exactly. Always cross-reference your memory map with the vendor's flash map documentation to ensure compatibility and avoid runtime crashes due to address conflicts or overflow.
Perfect, got it. I'll review the memory.x file again now.
Great. Let me know if you run into any issues during the build process.
